topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the canvas, the charcoal dust clinging to her fingertips like fine, dry silt. The piece, a sprawling depiction of fractured light, felt heavy in the studio air. She traced a line with her thumb, a familiar, almost mechanical motion. Then she noticed it—a tiny, almost invisible mark near the lower corner, a deliberate placement of ink that didn't belong to her own process. A slow, cool sensation crept up her neck. She leaned closer, squinting, her brow furrowing slightly, a flicker of something unreadable crossing her features before she simply pulled back, wiping her hands on a rag with practiced disinterest. It was just a smudge, she told herself. Another anomaly in the chaos of creation.

[story 2]

Marcus shuffled through the gallery, his gaze sweeping over the vibrant oil paintings with the practiced detachment of someone viewing furniture. He paused before a particularly intense portrait, the subject's eyes seeming to challenge him. As he approached, his eyes snagged on the wrist of the model. There, stark against the pale skin, was a complex, almost unsettling tattoo mirroring a motif from Marcus’s own recent sketches. A strange tightness settled in his chest, a dull pressure behind his ribs. He didn't stop to examine it closely; instead, he turned his attention back to the frame, his shoulders slumping just a fraction. "Odd," he murmured, the word sounding flat, devoid of real curiosity. He moved on, the image already dissolving into the background noise of the exhibition.

[story 3]

For Anya, the studio was just a space, a collection of tools and muted colors. She was mixing cadmium yellow, the viscosity of the paint a comforting, predictable thing. When she finally stepped back to assess the finished landscape, her attention snagged on a subtle detail on the edge of the canvas. It was a symbol, intricately drawn, undeniably hers, yet executed with a precision that felt borrowed. A slow, almost imperceptible tightening occurred around her jaw. She didn't move her head to look again. Instead, she picked up a palette knife, its weight familiar and grounding, and began scraping a thick layer of blue across the surface, deliberately obscuring the spot. It was a minor distraction, nothing more.
